title: Olist Customer Intelligence & Churn Prevention
subtitle: 97% of customers buy only once — an end-to-end ML pipeline to predict who’s about to leave, segment who’s worth saving, and quantify the cost of doing nothing.

sections.title: 1. Data Preparation
sections.body: We work with 5 interconnected Olist datasets (orders, customers, items, payments, products). After merging into a master table of 115K delivered orders, we see a right-skewed spending distribution — median R$120 vs mean R$160 — and identify the top revenue-driving product categories.
sections.snippet:
orders    = pd.read_csv("olist_orders_dataset.csv")
customers = pd.read_csv("olist_customers_dataset.csv")
items     = pd.read_csv("olist_order_items_dataset.csv")
payments  = pd.read_csv("olist_order_payments_dataset.csv")
products  = pd.read_csv("olist_products_dataset.csv")

delivered = orders[orders["order_status"] == "delivered"].copy()
df = (delivered
      .merge(customers, on="customer_id", how="left")
      .merge(items,     on="order_id",   how="left")
      .merge(payments,  on="order_id",   how="left")
      .merge(products[["product_id","product_category_name"]], on="product_id", how="left"))

sections.title: 2. Revenue & Order Trends
sections.body: Monthly GMV grew strongly through 2017–2018, peaking before a slight correction. Order volume tracks closely. The growth rate was flattening — signalling that organic customer acquisition alone won’t sustain momentum without improving retention.
sections.snippet:
gmv = df.groupby("order_month")["payment_value"].sum()
peak_idx = gmv.values.argmax()
axes[0].annotate(
    f"Peak: R${gmv.values[peak_idx]:,.0f}",
    xy=(peak_idx, gmv.values[peak_idx]),
    xytext=(peak_idx-3, gmv.values[peak_idx]*1.08),
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=PALETTE[3])
)
orders_m = df.groupby("order_month")["order_id"].nunique()

sections.title: 3. Repeat Purchase Behaviour
sections.body: This is the most critical finding: 97%+ of customers buy only once. The red bar dwarfs all others. Monthly active customer count also shows limited re-engagement over time. This single insight drives the entire downstream strategy — segmentation, churn modelling, and retention ROI.
sections.snippet:
customer_stats = df.groupby("customer_unique_id").agg(
    n_orders=("order_id", "nunique"),
    total_spend=("payment_value", "sum"),
    first_purchase=("order_purchase_timestamp", "min"),
    last_purchase=("order_purchase_timestamp", "max")
)
repeat_rate = (customer_stats["n_orders"] > 1).mean()
print(f"Repeat purchase rate: {repeat_rate*100:.1f}%")
print(f"{(customer_stats['n_orders']==1).sum():,} customers never came back")

sections.title: 4. RFM Customer Segmentation
sections.body: RFM scores customers on Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value (1–5 quintiles each), then assigns them to six segments. The R-F heatmap confirms heavy concentration in low-R/low-F — first-time buyers who never returned. Champions and Loyal customers are a small but disproportionately valuable group.
sections.snippet:
snapshot_date = df["order_purchase_timestamp"].max() + timedelta(days=1)
rfm = df.groupby("customer_unique_id").agg(
    Recency=("order_purchase_timestamp", lambda x: (snapshot_date - x.max()).days),
    Frequency=("order_id", "nunique"),
    Monetary=("payment_value", "sum")
)
rfm["R_score"] = pd.qcut(rfm["Recency"],  5, labels=[5,4,3,2,1])
rfm["F_score"] = pd.qcut(rfm["Frequency"].rank(method="first"), 5, labels=[1,2,3,4,5])

sections.title: 5. Optimal Cluster Count
sections.body: Before fitting K-Means, we use the elbow method and silhouette scores to select K. K=4 gives the best balance — inertia drops sharply up to K=4, then flattens, while silhouette peaks around K=4, indicating well-separated, compact clusters.
sections.snippet:
K_range = range(2, 9)
inertias, sil_scores = [], []
for k in K_range:
    km = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42, n_init=10)
    km.fit(X_scaled)
    inertias.append(km.inertia_)
    sil_scores.append(silhouette_score(X_scaled, km.labels_, sample_size=10000))
    print(f"K={k}: Inertia={km.inertia_:,.0f}, Silhouette={sil_scores[-1]:.4f}")

sections.title: 6. Cluster Profiles vs. RFM Rules
sections.body: K=4 produces four interpretable clusters: Champions, Loyalists, Mid-Tier, and Dormant. The cross-tabulation shows where rule-based RFM segments and ML clusters agree or diverge — high agreement validates business intuition; disagreements reveal nuanced sub-groups that rules miss.
sections.snippet:
km_final = KMeans(n_clusters=4, random_state=42, n_init=10)
km_final.fit(X_scaled)
rfm["Cluster"] = km_final.labels_

crosstab = pd.crosstab(
    rfm["Segment"],
    rfm["Cluster_Name"],
    normalize="index"
)
crosstab.plot(kind="bar", stacked=True, ax=axes[1],
              colormap="Set2", alpha=0.85)

sections.title: 7. Cohort Retention Heatmap
sections.body: Cohort analysis tracks each monthly acquisition group over time. Month-2 retention averages under 5% — confirming the single-purchase crisis is structural, not seasonal. No cohort shows meaningful improvement, pointing to systemic gaps in post-purchase engagement.
sections.snippet:
cohort_month = df.groupby("customer_unique_id")["order_month"].min()
df["cohort_month"] = df["customer_unique_id"].map(cohort_month)
df["cohort_index"] = (
    (df["order_month"].dt.year  - df["cohort_month"].dt.year) * 12
  + (df["order_month"].dt.month - df["cohort_month"].dt.month) + 1
)
cohort_pivot = cohort_data.pivot_table(
    index="cohort_month", columns="cohort_index",
    values="customer_unique_id"
)
retention = cohort_pivot.divide(cohort_size, axis=0)

sections.title: 8. Customer Lifetime Value
sections.body: CLV is computed as AOV × purchase frequency. The distribution is heavily right-skewed — most customers have a CLV close to their single order value. Repeat buyers are worth 3–4× more on average, making them the primary retention target.
sections.snippet:
clv = df.groupby("customer_unique_id").agg(
    total_orders=("order_id", "nunique"),
    avg_order_value=("payment_value", "mean")
)
clv["CLV"] = clv["avg_order_value"] * clv["total_orders"]

repeat_clv = clv[clv["total_orders"] > 1]
print(f"All customers  — Median CLV: R${clv['CLV'].median():.0f}")
print(f"Repeat buyers  — Median CLV: R${repeat_clv['CLV'].median():.0f}")
print(f"Repeat buyers are {repeat_clv['CLV'].mean()/clv['CLV'].mean():.1f}x more valuable")

sections.title: 9. Churn Prediction Model
sections.body: Churn is defined as no purchase in the last 90 days of the observation window. A Random Forest (AUC 0.84) outperforms Logistic Regression (AUC 0.76). The confusion matrix and feature importance reveal that Recency is by far the strongest predictor — customers inactive >60 days are at high risk.
sections.snippet:
cutoff_date = df["order_purchase_timestamp"].max() - timedelta(days=90)
churn_labels["is_churned"] = (churn_labels["last_purchase"] < cutoff_date).astype(int)

rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, max_depth=10,
    random_state=42, class_weight="balanced", n_jobs=-1)
rf.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

auc_rf = roc_auc_score(y_test, rf.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:,1])
print(f"Random Forest AUC: {auc_rf:.4f}")

sections.title: 10. Revenue Concentration (Pareto)
sections.body: The Lorenz curve confirms a strong Pareto effect: the top 20% of customers generate the majority of total revenue. Prioritising retention of this cohort — via targeted campaigns triggered by the churn model — yields the highest ROI with the smallest campaign footprint.
sections.snippet:
sorted_users  = user_spending.sort_values(ascending=False)
cum_revenue   = sorted_users.cumsum() / sorted_users.sum()
cum_customers = np.arange(1, len(sorted_users)+1) / len(sorted_users)
top_20_pct    = int(len(sorted_users) * 0.2)
pareto_ratio  = sorted_users.iloc[:top_20_pct].sum() / sorted_users.sum() * 100
print(f"Top 20% customers generate {pareto_ratio:.1f}% of revenue")
